speaker: interviewee
text: My Aunt Paula. We used to call her [NON-ENGLISH]. She-- her, her-- [NON-ENGLISH] Prensky. Her maiden name was Prensky. And of course, she was a [NON-ENGLISH] born. And she was truly a remarkable woman. She was truly like a saint.

speaker: interviewer
text: She-- can you imagine how, how she risked her life? Because any time a Jewish person stuck his nose out, they were, most of them-- people were killed right in the streets. Because there was an edict out that no Jews are permitted to go out in the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she promised my parents and had this-- well, not out of obligation, but out of sensitivity and, you know, family-- that she was the one who rounded up all the parents and-- through the Red Cross-- and said, we've got to do something to bring the children back. And so, when we came back, you know, to the house-- and she-- and we were already in the-- well, at her house, I think. She brought me to her house, which was in Kaunas, Kovno. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain the difference between the terms Kaunas and Kovno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, Kaunas is the Lithuanian name. In Lithuanian language, everything is ending with A-S or E-N-E. It's a different grammar. And in, in-- amongst the Jewish people or in Yiddish, we used to say Kovno. So-- but it's the same. In those days, Kovno was the capital of Lithuania. And Poland-- I mean Vilnius-- at one time belonged to Lithuania. But then I guess in 1929 when Lithuania gained its independence, Poland-- Vilnius went to Poland. And so then in 1944 when the Russians came back to Lithuania, they annexed Vilnius to Lithuania rather than to Poland. So now Vilnius is the capital of Lithuania. However, in those days when I was there, Kovno was the capital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, my other aunt's name was Chassia Yashuner. And my uncle was Osher Yashuner. And so-- and they had-- Osher had-- Osher and Chassia had one son, Monya. And he was about four in those days, maybe even two, three years old. My Aunt [NON-ENGLISH] had a son, Wolf. And he was the same age as I was. He was born in December, 1929, Wolf Prensky. And then she also had two older daughters. One is Genia, Genia, who was, who is about seven years older than I, than I am. And she's right now in, in-- she lives in New York. And I'm very close to her. She's truly like my sister. Then they had another one, another daughter. My Aunt Paula had another daughter. And her name was Liena. She was an Olympic runner, and she was killed in the ghetto right in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came back from camp, from summer camp, my aunt told me about-- my Aunt Paula [NON-ENGLISH] told me about what happened to my parents. And I remember-- I was 11 at the time. I remember crying, shedding a tear, or crying and then making up my mind that it was going to be OK, that I was going to survive. Were any of these children, your, your cousins, at camp with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I was all by myself, you know, with my friends, you know, naturally. So this was-- this is how it started. I was all started by myself and determined that whatever happens-- I had a feeling that perhaps God punished me. That since I was always so stubborn and I always wanted to run away from home, perhaps it was something like a punishment that I was left all by myself. Of course, I know now that, you know, it's not-- you know, that's [NON-ENGLISH]. But nevertheless, I mean, that feeling haunted me. But at the same time, I said it's, you know, that it's an adventure, and life is really an adventure. And, and it's-- whatever happens will be OK. I really believe that there was a, a fate and that we cannot fight it. And I should go along but at the same time look out for myself. And as I went on into different periods of my life, I, I blocked out things, and I put things behind because I said, I have to move on. And as things came up, I didn't really fight them, but I said-- but I didn't fight to change it. But at the same time, I fought the survival instinct. And within about two months, we were-- even in months, we were placed in the ghetto, which was, I would say, about 10 minutes. It was across a bridge, I remember. And we all were with wagons and-- I don't think-- maybe cars. We went into this place, into this ghetto. There was a barbed wire that was built around it. I do remember that. I remember there was a [NON-ENGLISH] ghetto and a, and a [NON-ENGLISH] ghetto. We were at first in the [NON-ENGLISH] ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do those words mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, [NON-ENGLISH] ghetto is-- means small and [NON-ENGLISH] means big. And so we-- I went to live with my Aunt [NON-ENGLISH] with her, her three children, Wolf, Genia, and Liena, and also with my Uncle Osher, Aunt Chassia, and [NON-ENGLISH], their little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what was the living arrangement? What did the place look like that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a small house, as I remember. I think we lived on the second story. And it was, consisted, I think, of one room with a kitchen. Well, the house was quite large. But there were different families living in the same house. But perhaps we lived at the time in two, three rooms. My Uncle Osher was also quite a survivor. And he was, as we used to call him, [NON-ENGLISH], he knew the land of the-- the, the layout of the land. And he would always [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] accommodate us with whatever [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] better-- we were really, I would say, more privileged than other people. So as I recall, we never were hungry for basic food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you think he managed this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was an administrator. And he knew-- he was also a very good public relations person. And that was his forte. He was very vocal, and he was very articulate and learned. I would say my whole family was really-- learning was one of the-- studies were, were, was one of the backbone of our family, really. My mother was already a college graduate. My father was a college graduate. My uncle was a doctor. He was-- I think everyone in my family was, you know, educated, had higher education. My aunt was sent to Paris to study because she was always very arty, and they wanted her to-- I remember my grandmother saying that she was a very beautiful lady, and I would always admire her. She would come from Paris with suitcases with all the latest clothing and everything. But my, my, my grandmother always groomed her to be a princess. She never says, oh, you don't have to put your hands in the, in the, in any water, you know. You'll have servants, which, incidentally, that's how I was brought up, too. We were not even permitted to dress ourselves until age-- I can't even remember. Everything was laid out, and the governess would come in, and you have to do this, and you have to do that. And take us to school with our little lunch pail. And I just rebelled. I was really-- always rebellion. Well, anyhow, so we were living in the-- we were marched into the ghetto. And within about two months, there was the [NON-ENGLISH], what we used to call them. They dug, in the seventh fort or ninth fort, they dug great big holes. And they immediately drove all of us out into the street, into the street in the [NON-ENGLISH] in the ghetto. And the Germans-- I remember them to this day-- went around and said, right, left, right, left. Right meant survival, and left meant death. My, my cousin Liena Prensky, Paula's, Aunt Paula's daughter was immediately-- my aunt was a very smart and bright woman. And she thought, well, perhaps she would send this 20-year-old with her boyfriend. She had a boyfriend, and they were both pictures of health. And she thought perhaps they would be spared, you know, to see two young, beautiful people. Of course, they needed them for work. But unfortunately, it, it was, did not happen. They, they were standing in line. So she sent my cousin Liena off with her boyfriend to stand in the lineup. And they were separated, and the whole family was killed together with my 20-year-old cousin. And my Aunt [NON-ENGLISH] never forgave herself for that. So that was a big blow in our family, because she was really the first one and, and, I must say, the only one who was killed during the ghetto years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at the time of this selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were all outside. And I remember standing with my Uncle Osher and with the whole family right there with the children. But my uncle somehow found out right away which was, was death and which one was life. And so we were alerted right away. So we made sure that we were separated, you know, into life. And so-- however, they did-- the Germans did, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] separate us into the, you know, right side, where I suppose we was life. And then after, oh, about-- this went on for, I think, a whole day. And after that day, we were told that we could go back to our houses and that the other people were just going to be evacuated to another city for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- I think it was, like-- it was the big Aktion. And I think it took place in October of 1941-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so October or November. And as I told you, I'm not very good in dates. Because one of the reasons of my survival, as I mentioned to you, was to block out things as I went along. So I didn't want to really remember bad things. I just moved on and said, OK, it's-- let's see what, what comes ahead of me. So then later on that night, we started hearing gunshots. And the next day or a couple days later, some people who were thrown into the graves, into to the holes, really-- they just-- they lined them up-- told us what had happened. And they were given up as dead. They just lined up all these people, 10,000 people around a, a hole. And there were machine guns. They just shot them all. And the Germans were standing-- actually, it was the Lithuanians who were standing right there and just threw them in. Whoever was dead just fell in. And whoever was not dead, they would just push them into that grave and then put dirt around it, on top of it. And they were still hearing the cries of the, of the people. So a few escaped. They just dug their way out of the graves and came back to Kovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this killing--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- it was right--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in relation to where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was right within half an hour. I think it was the ninth fort. I went to visit it, as a matter of fact. It's a memorial right now. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean, ninth fort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it was a, a fort-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at one time, it was built as a fort to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to arm themselves against enemies, you know, and-- because Lithuania was always being invaded by one country to another. And so it was just a perfect spot for, you know, for silence. And they had lots of room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they dug the graves right there, the big hole. And many, many Jews were-- as well as Lithuanians from what I understand-- were killed right there. May I add, also, that from what I, from what I remember is that the Lithuanians were even more cold-blooded murderers than the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the atrocities that went on, the Germans couldn't even bear looking at it whereas the Lithuanians were immediately with vengeance after the Jews, especially. And I remember them saying that Hitler opened our eyes up, that our eyes were closed. Because most of the Jewish population in Lithuania, in Kovno, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were quite well off, and we lived quite a very good life. There was, from what I understand, the Lithuanian, the Jewish population really had-- was the enlightenment of Europe in-- for the Jews in, in Kovno, especially-- or in Lithuania, there were many yeshivas. There were many Jewish printings. The Yiddish in Lithuania was the literary Yiddish, many learned people and, and rabbis. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see of this Lithuanian behavior towards the Jews in the ghetto or in this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were very, very rough and very vengeful. They immediately-- when we were placed into the ghetto, they immediately vandalized all the homes. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most of-- I wouldn't say all the Lithuanians, but most of the Lithuanians were really cold-blooded murderers. Many of them were really-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] felt very badly about it and, and would come over. I remember they would come to the barbed wires, and they would start trading with us and, and bringing us food and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: People that you knew from your past?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we, we made-- the only way you could go out of the ghetto was in a working force. And so they used to take them out as laborers, hard laborers. And so they would go out in the morning, and then they'd come back in the evening. And, and so the Lithuanians would come over to the workforce and especially to trade, you know, for goods and valuables. And, you know, some of them would, would send letters with the, with, with people, say do you know so and so, and please give them this letter, you know, and sympathy and so on and asking them if they could help in any way. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any of these situations yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't because I was only 11. And so I was not-- I could not go out to work. There was, there were edicts out that you could-- laws that you could only go out in the working force, I think, 16 or 18 years old. You had to be 16 or 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, I was 11, and we went into, as I said, the ghetto. Well, when-- the Aktions, the Aktions, where they separated people to-- and killed them off were going on. But the big one was only-- you know, and as I said, I think it was November or-- and thereafter, there were small separations and killings. Did you encounter any yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I encountered them separate-- you know, drive-- every time we were driven out of our homes into the streets. And the Germans would come through and look through who, who-- especially they pick the ones who were old and sick. You know, they would separate them immediately and kill them off, take them out and-- again, under the camouflage that they were just sending them to better headquarters for better care, to hospitals and so on. Well, then eventually, within, I would say, half a year, we were-- the, the population, the Jewish population dwindled. And so we were put into the big ghetto. And the, the small ghetto was, was closed off, partitioned off, because they didn't need so much room. And I remember then we went to live in another house in the big ghetto. And that house I remember quite distinctly was downstairs. We had-- there was a kitchen immediately upon entrance. To the right was my Aunt Paula. There was a bedroom there, and she lived there with her two children because one was-- had died in the big Aktion. And then to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] past the kitchen was another room. And in that room was my uncle and my aunt-- Uncle Osher and Chassia their little boy, [NON-ENGLISH] in a, in a bed-- in a crib, actually. So he must've been about a year and a half at the time, now that I think about it, or two in a crib. And right next to his bed was my bed. And I think it was a roll-away bed. And then we even took in another Polish person from Lodz, I think. He was, he was in the Lithuanian ghetto, too-- Zietzev. And so he was living with us as well. So here we were, five people living, you know, in one little room. And we had a kitchen. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to have enough food? You said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were, as I said, quite fortunate. My uncle always saw to it that we had food. My-- and my cousin Gertrude who is now in New York, she was a beautiful girl. She was-- I think at the time, she was 19. And she would go out into the workforce.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- and she also was a very directed and very survivor type. And so she would always make sure that she-- and good salesperson-- that, you know, she would bring back sausages. I remember long, big sausages and bread and chickens.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we, I would say, as compared to the other people in the ghetto, we were quite fortunate. Of course, there was no schooling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Starting in 1941 when they drove us into the ghetto, schools were forbidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here again, I said, well, that was fine. I, I liked school, but not that much. And so I said, well, great.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- immediately, they started organizing craft schools. And it was-- I think ORT had-- somehow, they had gotten money from ORT to establish schools. And the Germans did permit that on the pretext that we were going to be-- that they were going to teach us a trade that would benefit the, the workforce.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in a sewing class. My cousin Wolf went with me, and he was in carpentry and plumbing. We would take various classes but nothing of educational nature, nothing of, you know, of math or, or history or languages-- nothing like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were only permitted to do trade teachings. So that was not far from the house. I remember we used to go over there. And things were-- every day was an eventful day because there were all kinds of happenings every single day as far as, as far as separating people and dwindling-- you know, killing off people. And there was the [NON-ENGLISH] and the big Aktion and the [NON-ENGLISH], children's Aktion, old people Aktion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe something that you saw, you witnessed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I remember that in our house-- actually, under my Aunt Paula's bedroom, under a couch, there was what we called a [NON-ENGLISH], a, a hiding place, that my uncle built, my Uncle Osher. And what it consisted of was we would pull the couch out. And there was a great big cover. And under the cover was like a little basement, a little hole in the ground. And we had it all equipped with food, with water, and some food and light-- flashlights. And so whenever there was a rumor out that there was another Aktion, another separation in, in the works, we would many times go into this [NON-ENGLISH], into this hiding place. And we would stay there, sometimes for several days until, until the panic was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you could fit in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was big enough for about eight people. So it was quite large. And so that many times saved us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times did you go into that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many times. Because sometimes, it was just a rumor. So we would go in for a few hours. And sometimes we would have to stay there for several days. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it just your family that went in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was just our family. And of course, it was supposed to be secretive. Because no one knew about it. And it was, it was so well hidden that you really would never suspect that there was one because the couch would just-- it was a big couch. And that was over that-- over that hole. So it was so designed that we could close it off from the inside. And we could open it from the inside. It was-- I think it was on springs. It was really very cleverly engineered.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was mainly our life consisted. I remember there was no entertainment. We would-- there were, you know, no movies, no-- nothing like that. There were no concerts. So-- well, there was a symphony, I, I understand, that was, that was there and, again, on the pretext that it was-- they were-- we were practicing for the Germans, you know, for their benefit.

speaker: interviewee
text: But otherwise, there were-- we weren't permitted. There were no gatherings permitted. The Germans were-- had a pretty good, tight fist, and the Lithuanians, on us. So this went on until about 1940-- 1943, the end of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your health during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My health was really pretty good. I was strong, and I was tall. I was as-- almost as tall as I am now, which is about 5'1 and 1/2, 5'2. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my, my aunts treated me very nicely, my family-- my aunts and uncles. And as I said, it was Aunt Paula who was-- I, I remember there was-- in those days, perhaps even parents did not believe in really shedding, in really giving children all kinds of hugs and kisses and love and, and saying, repeating that I love you or whatever. So it was-- I, I was treated by my aunts very well. But of course, I-- since I was a very independent child, they would also look over my shoulders and not permit me to play with older children where I was really interested in sexual adventures-- I mean, you know, to get books.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember we had a little library, and so I took out a book on sex. And God forbid, it was forbidden, you know? I was at the time already 12, 13 years old. So I would have to read it. I remember at night look through all the pictures. And we would go into huddles later on during the day with other friends and say, did you hear-- do you know about this? Do you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was just-- it was just passe, you know-- a penis or, God forbid, anybody would say that word or a vagina or anything like that. So-- or, you know, we would go into huddles how children are born or, you know, what happens when you get married and so on, so forth. No one ever told us anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember one day, I read the book at night with a flashlight. And I put it under the bed. And in the morning, I got up and went to school. But when I came back, my Aunt Paula and [NON-ENGLISH] alerted me-- or, you know, the minute I came in, I could see from their faces that I was in trouble. Well, they found the book under my bed.
